top_concerns.concern: Third-round motivation, bracket path, same-time scoreboard pressure, and market sentiment are all present in the chain already. They must not be stacked again in summary language or manual probability nudges.
top_concerns.evidence: data-modeler.json already includes third_round_context, qualification_scenarios, motivation_profile, bracket_path_expectation, rotation_risk, strategic_tempo_adjustment, and market_adjustment for both matches.
top_concerns.why_it_matters: Double counting can overfit Japan draw management, Sweden must-win aggression, and Netherlands incentive edge.
top_concerns.follow_up_check: Keep only one explicit third-round adjustment layer in final writeup and in any manual red-team probability trim.

top_concerns.concern: Japan vs Sweden favorite framing is still vulnerable to overstating Japan control because ordinary 1X2 supports Japan, but the same chain also shows Sweden late-game and set-piece tails remain live.
top_concerns.evidence: Quant output has Japan 46.37, draw 25.99, Sweden 27.63, while market implied is 50.92, 26.06, 23.01 and handicap -1 does not support an easy Japan margin.
top_concerns.why_it_matters: If summary language follows brand or market favorite bias, Sweden's must-win aerial and set-piece path gets underweighted.
top_concerns.follow_up_check: Keep Sweden upset and draw tails explicit; do not market Japan as a clean-control favorite.

top_concerns.concern: Tunisia vs Netherlands still lacks ordinary 1X2 lottery pricing, so the market calibration chain is weaker and +2 handicap should not be converted into a high-confidence away-win endorsement.
top_concerns.evidence: data-collector.json marks ordinary_status unopened; quant output uses handicap +2 only and keeps market_adjustment weight lower for this match.
top_concerns.why_it_matters: Without ordinary 1X2, the model can still project Netherlands on strength, but precision on away-win and big-margin tails should be reduced.
top_concerns.follow_up_check: Retain weaker calibration warning and avoid presenting Netherlands win rate as tightly locked.

top_concerns.concern: Official T-75 lineups, final injuries, and card accumulation status remain unresolved for both matches.
top_concerns.evidence: data-collector.json and tactics-coach.json both keep official lineups unavailable and discipline or medical refresh incomplete.
top_concerns.why_it_matters: Lineup and workload uncertainty directly affect xG bands, rotation assumptions, and late-match substitution scripts.
top_concerns.follow_up_check: Refresh before any upgrade from discussion_only wording.

top_concerns.concern: Third-place and bracket-path language is still heuristic rather than fully official-state complete.
top_concerns.evidence: quant output explicitly says later FIFA tiebreakers are not modeled and third-place advancement is heuristic because other groups are external dependencies.
top_concerns.why_it_matters: Overstating these probabilities as exact can mislead the motivation story, especially for Tunisia's theoretical-only path and Sweden's best-third route.
top_concerns.follow_up_check: Mark third-place advancement and bracket-path effects as heuristic support, not exact qualification math.

upset_or_failure_scenarios.scenario: If Netherlands lead early in the parallel match, Japan can become more draw-protective and Sweden's direct-play or set-piece tail grows in the last 30 minutes.
If Sweden starts a more aggressive front-foot version than estimated, Japan's wing circulation and buildup control can be disrupted earlier than the median script.
If Dutch rotation is heavier than assumed, Netherlands can still be the better team while the away-win margin tail compresses toward 0-1, 0-2, or 1-1 type outcomes.
If Tunisia concedes early again, collapse tail remains live and independent Poisson may still understate the messy high-variance branch.

overconfidence_flags: Do not treat Japan ordinary 1X2 1.74 as proof that the qualitative read is solved.
Do not sell Netherlands as a full-confidence market-backed away favorite while ordinary 1X2 is unopened.
Do not write Tunisia's path as live qualification equity; it is theoretical-only and near-elimination in practical terms.
Do not present third-place advancement probabilities as exact official-state outputs.
market_flags: Japan vs Sweden: ordinary 1X2 favors Japan, but handicap -1 does not support a relaxed big-margin script.
Tunisia vs Netherlands: ordinary 1X2 unavailable, so handicap +2 only constrains blowout interpretation and does not fully calibrate away-win pricing.
Because the lottery market set is incomplete, any market-reference sentence must state that calibration is partial.
quant_chain_flags: Japan already has lower model win probability than market; avoid additional manual anti-Sweden discounting.
Netherlands strength edge is real, but away-win precision should be wider because market and lineup confirmation are incomplete.
Independent Poisson still underweights red cards, early goals, late chasing, and collapse branches.
Later FIFA tiebreakers and best-third advancement remain heuristic in the current simulation layer.

japan_vs_sweden.note: This is mainly a calibration guardrail, not a demand for a large rewrite.

tunisia_vs_netherlands.note: The concern is not that Netherlands should stop being favorite, but that the current precision and margin confidence are still too neat.

must_fix_before_publish.item: State clearly that third-place advancement and bracket-path effects are heuristic, not full official-state exact math.
Do not add another manual motivation boost or market boost on top of the existing third_round_context and market_adjustment layers.
For Japan vs Sweden, preserve Sweden must-win plus set-piece or aerial threat in the final narrative and avoid clean-favorite wording for Japan.
For Tunisia vs Netherlands, explicitly mark ordinary 1X2 as unavailable and prevent handicap +2 from being narrated as a full market endorsement.
Keep T-75 lineups, final injuries, and card accumulation status marked unresolved at publish time unless refreshed.

handoff_notes: Prediction can be published only as discussion_only_pre_lineup while verdict remains revise.
The main issues are calibration and disclosure, not a full rebuild of the F-group chain.
Japan vs Sweden needs anti-favorite-overstatement language; Tunisia vs Netherlands needs anti-false-market-certainty language.
